title: The Reputation That Gets You Hired
paras: The Reputation That Gets You Hired. This is a recruiter's guide to building a profile, a C-V, and a presence that makes employers want to recruit you — for planners at every stage, here in the United Kingdom, in 2026.

Most people think recruitment begins the moment they send a C-V. After more than thirty years placing planners — from graduates to directors — I can tell you the truth is it begins long before that, and it never really stops. By the time your application lands, your name has been searched, your LinkedIn read, and any appeal decisions, talks, or online comments seen too. The C-V isn't the start of the process. It's the moment a picture that already exists gets confirmed — or contradicted.

That's not meant to alarm you. It's meant to reframe how you think about your career. You are not a document, assessed a few times a decade when you happen to be job-hunting. You are a reputation — built continuously, existing online whether you tend it or not, and consulted by employers and recruiters before they ever speak to you. The planners who get the best jobs, and the fastest promotions, are rarely the ones with the cleverest C-V tricks. They're the ones whose reputation does the work before they walk in the room.
One note before we start: the figures in this guide on U-K recruiters, and what they do online before contact, come from StandOut C-V, in 2025.

title: Part 1 — Building your professional brand
paras: Part one — building your professional brand. Now, the word 'brand' makes a lot of planners wince, because it sounds like marketing — and planners are, rightly, suspicious of marketing. So let me define it plainly. Your professional brand is simply the reputation that arrives before you do. It's what people say, think, and expect about you when your name comes up and you're not in the room. It isn't a logo or a slogan. It's the answer to the question a recruiter or employer quietly asks about every candidate: what is this person known for, and can I trust them?

Everyone already has one. The graduate who's always prepared has a brand. The senior planner known for winning difficult appeals has one. The officer whose reports are a pleasure to read has one — and so does the one whose reports are late and thin. A brand isn't something you either have or lack. It's something you either shape, or leave to chance. The only real choice is whether the reputation that precedes you is the one you would have chosen. Most people never think about it, which means most people's brand is an accident. Yours doesn't have to be.

First impressions form fast — and increasingly, they form online. In U-K surveys of recruiters, around ninety-one percent use LinkedIn regularly, and around eighty-four percent screen candidates on social platforms. That's StandOut C-V, 2025. The first impression you make is usually not your handshake, or even your C-V. It's your LinkedIn photo, your headline, the tone of your last few posts, and whatever a search of your name returns. Those things are judged in seconds, and the judgement colours everything that follows.

And why is trust the whole game? Because underneath a brand sits one thing: trust. Recruiters place — and employers hire and promote — people they trust to do the work, represent them well, and not become a problem. Everything in this guide — the honest C-V, the consistent LinkedIn presence, the clean digital footprint, the reputation for helping colleagues — is really about building that trust, and removing reasons to doubt it. A career is a long game of accumulated trust, and the planners who understand that early move faster, and further.

title: Part 2 — Writing an outstanding CV
paras: Part two — writing an outstanding C-V. The C-V still matters — it's where your reputation gets evidenced — so it's worth getting right. An excellent planning C-V isn't hard to write. It just takes discipline, honesty, and the avoidance of a few common mistakes.

Let's start with length, layout, and the look of it. For almost everyone, two pages is the target. A graduate can manage on one or two, and only a genuinely senior planner with a long record needs a third. Keep the layout clean and conventional: a clear name and contact line, a short profile, then employment history in reverse order, education, qualifications, and skills. Use a plain, readable font — Calibri, Arial, Georgia, or similar — at a sensible size, with generous but not wasteful margins, and consistent formatting. Use colour sparingly. A single restrained accent for headings is fine; a rainbow is not. Recruiters read hundreds of these, and clarity beats cleverness every time.

Next — P-D-F, Word, and the machines that read first. Send a P-D-F unless the employer or agency asks for Word, because a P-D-F preserves your formatting on any screen. The one caveat is the applicant tracking system — the A-T-S — the software many larger employers use to receive and search C-Vs. Exact U-K prevalence isn't reliably published, so treat that figure as uncertain. But the practical rule costs you nothing: make your C-V machine-readable. That means a clean, single-column layout. Standard section headings — 'Employment', 'Education'. No text inside images, graphics, text boxes, or headers and footers that a parser may miss. And the real keywords a search would use: 'chartered town planner', 'development management', 'M-R-T-P-I', 'Local Plan', the software you use. A beautiful C-V the machine cannot read never reaches the human.

Now, the sections, and what goes in them. A planning C-V should include: a short personal profile, three or four lines. Employment history focused on achievements. Education. Professional qualifications and memberships — R-T-P-I chartership, that's M-R-T-P-I, above all, plus R-I-C-S, I-C-E, or others where relevant, and your C-P-D. And a skills section. For planners, the skills worth naming specifically include G-I-S — and say which: Arc-G-I-S, Q-G-I-S — CAD where you use it, the Planning Portal and case-management systems, data analysis, and the substantive competencies employers search for: development management, planning policy, major applications, appeals, public inquiries, committee experience, and presentation skills. Name the real things you've done. They're also the keywords a recruiter searches on.

And here is the one rule that matters most: achievements, not duties. A weak C-V lists duties — the things your job description says you were responsible for. A strong C-V shows achievements — what you actually did, with a result and, wherever possible, a number. Every planner in a given role has the same duties. What separates you is what you achieved within them.

Let me give you some illustrative wording — and use your own real figures; never inflate them. Here's a 'before': 'A hardworking and motivated planner with excellent communication skills, looking for a new and exciting challenge where I can develop my career.' And here's the 'after': 'Chartered town planner, M-R-T-P-I, with seven years' development-management experience across major residential and commercial schemes, a strong appeal record, and a track record of steering contentious applications through committee. Now seeking a principal role in a growth-focused consultancy.' The first could be anyone. The second is a person a recruiter can place. Specificity is credibility.

title: Part 3 — The cover letter
paras: Part three — the cover letter. A cover letter, where one is asked for, should be one page — three or four short paragraphs — and it should do a different job from the C-V. The C-V is the evidence; the letter is the argument. It answers two questions the C-V cannot: why this employer, and why you, for this role, now.

A simple structure works. Open with why them — something specific and genuine that a template could not contain: a scheme they're working on, their reputation in a sector, their growth. Then why you — the two or three things about your experience that fit this role, with a concrete example, not a restatement of your C-V. Then a short close: your enthusiasm, your availability, and a confident sign-off. Never copy your C-V into prose. Never send a generic letter with the company name swapped in — recruiters spot it instantly, and a wrong company name left in from the last application is a genuine, common, career-ending howler. And never make it about what you want from them, rather than what you offer.

Here's the kind of thing I mean — imagine writing to a firm about one of their regeneration schemes, by name: 'I've followed your work on the scheme with real interest — strategic, contentious urban sites are exactly where I do my best work. Over the past four years I've taken three major residential applications from pre-application to consent, including one recovered at appeal, and I'm known for keeping difficult schemes moving and members onside. I'd relish bringing that to your growing development-management team, and I'm available at four weeks' notice.' Specific, evidenced, and about them as much as about you.

title: Part 4 — LinkedIn
paras: Part four — LinkedIn. For a modern professional, LinkedIn is not optional, because it's where recruiters and employers look first. Around ninety-one percent of recruiters use it regularly, and among specialist social recruiters, effectively all of them use it to search for candidates — StandOut C-V again, 2025. The U-K has some thirty million users. A strong profile is, in practice, a second C-V that works while you sleep — one recruiters find proactively, bringing opportunities to you rather than the other way round.

The first job is to be findable. Recruiters find candidates by searching keywords, so your profile has to contain the words they search for. Think what an employer looking for you would type — 'chartered town planner', 'development management', 'M-R-T-P-I', 'planning policy', your city — and make sure those words appear naturally in your headline, your about section, and your experience. The single most wasted piece of LinkedIn real estate is the headline, which most people leave as just their job title. Use it. Something like 'Chartered Town Planner, M-R-T-P-I — Development Management and Major Applications', plus your city, is human-readable and packed with the terms that make you findable. Your about section should be first person. It should tell a short professional story, carry your achievements and your keywords, and read like you — not a C-V pasted in.

A word on the extras. Recommendations are the most under-used feature on LinkedIn, and among the most persuasive — ask for them, specifically, after a job well done. Premium can help an active job-seeker, with InMail and insights, and Creator mode suits those who post regularly — but neither is necessary for a strong profile.

And remember — networking is being useful, not collecting contacts. Send connection requests with a short personal note rather than the blank default. It roughly doubles acceptance, and it starts a relationship. Follow the organisations and people who matter to your career: the R-T-P-I, the local authorities and consultancies you admire, and the planning leaders whose thinking you respect. When you message a recruiter, be concise and specific. A vague 'please find me a job' gets little. A targeted 'I'm an M-R-T-P-I development-management planner in the North West looking to move into consultancy — is that a market you work in?' gets a reply.

Now — posting, and the 2026 algorithm. You don't have to post to benefit, but a little thoughtful activity builds visibility and credibility over time. The algorithm changes, and no one outside LinkedIn knows it exactly, so treat this as informed guidance, not gospel. First: consistency beats volume. Posting two to five times a week, steadily, does more than a burst and then silence — and a comment counts too. Second: comments matter more than likes. The 2026 consensus is that a comment is worth roughly twice a like to your reach, because it signals real engagement. Third: dwell time and early engagement count. Content people actually stop and read, and that gets engagement in the first hour, travels furthest. And fourth: knowledge and stories perform; bait and links underperform. Genuine insight, a lesson from a case, a plain-English explainer, or native video do well. Engagement-bait, and posts whose main content is an external link, tend to be shown less.

The aim is credibility, not celebrity. Two examples of the kind of post that works for a planner. A short, honest reflection: 'An appeal I lost last month taught me more than any win — here's the point about heritage harm I'd underweighted, and how I'd argue it now.' Or a plain-English explainer: 'Biodiversity net gain in ninety seconds, for anyone who still finds it baffling.' Useful, human, and unmistakably from someone who knows the work.

title: Part 5 — Other social media
paras: Part five — other social media. LinkedIn is the one platform that genuinely matters for a planning career. The rest range from mildly useful to actively risky, and the honest answer to 'should I be on all of them?' is a firm no. Spread yourself across every platform, and you'll maintain none of them well — and expose yourself on several.

So here's the practical rule. Invest in LinkedIn. Keep a portfolio or a blog if it suits your specialism. And treat everything else as personal space to be secured, rather than a career tool. The other platforms far more often cost people opportunities than win them.

title: Part 6 — What employers check
paras: Part six — what employers check. Employers and recruiters do due diligence, and you should assume they do it on you. Around eighty-four percent screen candidates on social media, and twenty-one percent have rejected a candidate because of what they found — StandOut C-V, 2025. A typical check runs from the professional outward: your LinkedIn, then a plain Google of your name, then whatever public social media turns up. And for planners specifically, there are sources most professions don't have. Planning Inspectorate decisions name the planners and advocates involved, so your appeal record is partly public. R-T-P-I membership and professional disciplinary records can be checked. Companies House shows directorships. And for senior hires, employers may look at published papers, conference talks, podcast appearances, articles, and news coverage. Most of this, for a competent professional, is reassuring.

A word of fairness here. Good employers — and C-I-P-D guidance on selection — warn against over-reaching in social-media screening, because it can introduce bias and stray into personal life, and data-protection law applies. But you can't control how carefully any given employer draws that line, so the safe assumption is that anything public may be seen. The point is not to become bland or fearful. It's to make sure that what is public is professional, or innocuous. Political views, held and expressed civilly, are your right. A stream of aggressive, offensive, or extreme content is a different matter — and it does cost people jobs.

title: Part 7 — Your digital reputation
paras: Part seven — your digital reputation. The single most important thing to understand about your online life is this: nothing on it is ever completely private, and nothing is ever completely gone. A message can be screenshotted. A 'private' group has members. A deleted post may already have been captured. Old content resurfaces — the Facebook photos from a decade ago, the tweets you forgot, the things you posted at university before you had a profession to protect. Tagged photographs put you in other people's posts, outside your control — and your friends' privacy settings are not yours.

That's a reason for care, not panic. The sensible response is a periodic digital tidy-up. Search your own name, and see what an employer would see. Tighten privacy on personal accounts, so photos, posts, and tags are visible to friends, not the world. Review old content, and remove anything you wouldn't want a future manager, client, or committee to read — not to deceive, but because youthful nonsense doesn't represent the professional you now are. Untag yourself from anything unhelpful. And going forward, apply the oldest test before you post: would I be comfortable if this appeared on the front of the local paper, next to my name — and my employer's?

And what should you leave? Everything professional, thoughtful, or simply human and harmless. You're not trying to erase yourself, or become a faceless corporate cipher. A real person with genuine interests is more appealing than a blank one. Clean up the risks; keep the personality.

title: Part 8 — Interview preparation
paras: Part eight — interview preparation. If your reputation and your application get you to interview, preparation is what converts it. The planners who interview well are almost never the most naturally gifted talkers. They're the ones who prepared properly — and it shows. Research the employer thoroughly: their schemes, sectors, recent news, values. Research the interviewers on LinkedIn. Anticipate the questions, which for planners are often practical and case-based: talk me through a difficult application; a refusal you defended; how you deal with committee, or an angry objector; a policy point relevant to the role; and why you want this job. And prepare your own thoughtful questions about the role, the team, and the work — asking only about pay and leave does the opposite.

For competency questions, use the STAR technique. Answer with STAR: briefly set the Situation, the Task, the Action you took — that's the heart of it — and the Result. For example: 'We had a contentious sixty-home application with strong objection' — that's the situation. 'I had to secure a recommendation members could support' — the task. 'I reworked the scheme with the applicant, ran a members' briefing, and addressed the highways concern head-on' — the action. 'It was approved with cross-party support, and not appealed' — the result. Far more persuasive than 'I have good negotiation skills.'

Online interviews are their own small skill. A plain, tidy background. The camera at eye level — raise the laptop — so you're not looming, or looking down. Light on your face from in front, not a bright window behind you. A decent microphone, and a tested connection. And look into the camera, not at your own image. Dress as you would in person — smart, and err on the formal side.

title: Part 9 — Building a career
paras: Part nine — building a career. Getting hired is one thing; getting on is another. After thirty years watching careers unfold, I can tell you that promotions rarely go to whoever served longest. They go to the people who made themselves visible, valuable, and trusted — who deliver, who are known for something, and whom decision-makers can picture in the bigger role. Time served is the weakest of career strategies. Reputation is the strongest.

Recruiters and employers spot future leaders by a recognisable set of signals, and every one of them is within your control. Deliver, commercially and technically. Keep your C-P-D and chartership current — they are the licence to progress. Develop a specialism, and become genuinely expert in something scarce and valuable. The planner known as 'the person for energy consents', or 'the one who wins the difficult appeals', has a reputation that markets itself. Build visibility, by writing the occasional article, speaking at an event, sitting on a committee. Network genuinely, so your name is in the room when opportunities arise. And — the most underrated of all — help junior staff. The planners who mentor and lift others are remembered warmly, recommended widely, and trusted with leadership.

title: Part 10 — Salary progression
paras: Part ten — salary progression. Reputation doesn't only get you hired and promoted; it shows up directly in your pay. It's one reason two planners with identical experience on paper can earn markedly different salaries. The higher earner is usually not more technically able. They're more visible, more commercially valuable, better networked, and better at negotiating. A planner whose name a recruiter already knows, who's known for winning work or winning appeals, and who has options, holds cards the equally-experienced but invisible planner does not. Reputation is, quite literally, leverage.

Here are the headlines. Changing employer is usually the fastest route to a significant rise, because an external offer is benchmarked to the market, while an internal one is benchmarked to your current salary. Negotiate from evidence, and from your whole package — not from need — and don't be afraid to ask. In a candidate's market, employers expect it. Treat counter-offers with caution: they fix the money, but rarely the reasons you looked, and many who accept them have moved on within the year. Use a good recruiter, who knows the market rate and negotiates at arm's length. And mind the timing: the moment of maximum leverage is when you have an offer in hand, and they have decided they want you. The full mechanics are in PlanRef's Salaries and Progression guide.

title: Part 11 — Fifty mistakes that damage careers
paras: Part eleven — fifty mistakes that damage careers. I've ranked these roughly from the most serious — the things that end candidacies and careers — down to the smaller habits that quietly hold people back. Few people make many of these. Most make one or two without realising — which is exactly why they're worth hearing in full.

title: Most serious — these end candidacies and careers
paras: First, the most serious — these end candidacies and careers. Number one: lying on your C-V — inventing qualifications, dates, or experience. Found out, it ends the process, and the reputation. Two: misrepresenting your R-T-P-I or professional status — claiming chartership you don't hold. Checkable, and career-ending. Three: discriminatory, racist, or hateful content online — the single fastest way to be rejected, and stay rejected. Four: a professional disciplinary finding you concealed — the concealment is often worse than the finding. Five: aggressive or abusive public behaviour online — pile-ons, threats, sustained hostility. Employers see a liability.

Six: breaching confidentiality — posting about live cases, clients, or employers. Instantly disqualifying. Seven: badmouthing former employers, in interviews or online. It says more about you than about them. Eight: getting caught in a lie during interview — a single exposed exaggeration collapses all your other claims. Nine: extreme political content, conspiracy theories, or misinformation — it reads as poor judgement, the one thing a professional cannot lack. And ten: offensive 'humour' — the joke you thought was harmless, screenshotted and shared.

title: Serious — these regularly cost jobs and promotions
paras: Next, the serious ones — these regularly cost jobs and promotions. Eleven: no LinkedIn presence at all — in 2026, that's an absence that reads as hiding, or disengaged. Twelve: a LinkedIn profile that contradicts your C-V — mismatched dates or titles look like dishonesty. Thirteen: public alcohol, drug, or party imagery — the classic, and still one of the commonest, screen-outs. Fourteen: sending the identical generic C-V and cover letter to every role — recruiters can tell in seconds. Fifteen: leaving the wrong company name in a cover letter — the copy-paste tell that ends applications. Sixteen: a C-V that lists duties, not achievements — indistinguishable from every other applicant. Seventeen: typos and sloppy formatting — in a detail profession, they read as carelessness. Eighteen: turning up to interview having done no research — obvious, and unforgivable.

Nineteen: letting your chartership or C-P-D lapse — it closes the door to senior roles. Twenty: accepting a counter-offer for the money alone — and finding the reasons you left are still there. Twenty-one: burning bridges when you resign — planning is a small world with a long memory. Twenty-two: being unreachable, or slow to reply to a recruiter or employer — it reads as disinterest. Twenty-three: overstating seniority, or fabricating an impressive-sounding job title — checkable, and cheap. Twenty-four: a careless or unprofessional email address or voicemail — small, but noticed. And twenty-five: being rude to junior staff, receptionists, or recruiters — it always gets back.

title: Moderate — these quietly hold you back
paras: Then the moderate ones — these quietly hold you back. Twenty-six: never negotiating salary — leaving money on the table, year after year. Twenty-seven: staying invisible — never writing, speaking, or contributing to the profession. Twenty-eight: never developing a specialism — remaining a generalist when specialists are scarce, and paid more. Twenty-nine: waiting to be promoted rather than making the case — assuming time served is enough. Thirty: a weak or absent LinkedIn headline — wasting your prime keyword space. Thirty-one: not tailoring your profile or C-V to the direction you actually want to go. Thirty-two: neglecting your network until the moment you need it — by then, it's too late.

Thirty-three: refusing to help colleagues — being known as clever, but not generous. Thirty-four: no recommendations on LinkedIn — an easy, persuasive asset left unused. Thirty-five: ignoring commercial awareness — staying purely technical when leadership needs business sense. Thirty-six: poor interview storytelling — good work, badly told, because you never used STAR. Thirty-seven: a bad online-interview setup — dark, looming, glitchy — undercutting a strong candidate. Thirty-eight: only ever asking about pay and leave at interview — signalling the wrong priorities. Thirty-nine: never asking for feedback after a rejection — losing the one useful thing it offers. And forty: job-hopping with no narrative — frequent moves, with no story of progression.

title: Minor — the habits worth fixing
paras: And finally, the minor ones — the habits worth fixing. Forty-one: an outdated C-V you scramble to update only when you need it — always half a step behind. Forty-two: a blank LinkedIn banner and a poor photo — small signals of not bothering. Forty-three: connecting with the blank default note — a missed chance to start a relationship. Forty-four: over-formatting the C-V with graphics an A-T-S cannot read. Forty-five: listing every task you have ever done, rather than what mattered.

Forty-six: vague adjectives — 'passionate', 'dynamic', 'hardworking' — with nothing behind them. Forty-seven: never updating your profile after a promotion or a big project. Forty-eight: chasing LinkedIn virality instead of quiet credibility. Forty-nine: forgetting to proofread the very email you send the C-V with. And fifty: underselling yourself out of modesty — the quiet, common mistake of good planners who never quite say what they achieved.

title: Part 12 — The six-month career checklist
paras: Part twelve — the six-month career checklist. Careers are built in small, regular steps, not occasional panics. Twice a year — put it in the calendar — spend an hour working through this. It's the single most useful habit in this guide.

First, your C-V: updated with recent achievements — not duties — still two pages, still A-T-S-clean, still honest. Then LinkedIn: headline, about, and experience current; photo and banner good; a recent recommendation requested. Then C-P-D: logged and on track, a genuine knowledge gap identified, and a plan to fill it. Then membership: subscription paid, chartership progressing or maintained, record clean. Then networking: three genuine connections made or renewed, and one event or conversation in the diary. Then social media: your name searched, personal accounts locked down, nothing public you wouldn't stand behind.

Then interview skills: two fresh STAR examples from recent work, ready to tell. Then achievements: written down as they happen — keep a running list — so nothing is forgotten when it counts. Then career goals: reviewed — where do you want to be in two years, and is this six months moving you there? Then training: one skill or specialism deliberately advanced. Then mentoring: helping someone junior — and, whatever your stage, being mentored yourself. And finally, visibility: one thing done to be seen — a comment, an article, a talk, a committee — however small.

That's the whole of it, really. Do the work. Tell the truth. Be findable. Be generous. And keep the reputation that precedes you one you'd be proud to walk in behind. Do that steadily, over years, and you will spend far less of your career chasing jobs — because the good ones will come looking for you.

title: Sources and notes
paras: A few words on sources and notes, before we finish. The recruitment statistics come from StandOut C-V — U-K recruitment and social-media recruiting statistics for 2024 to 2025: ninety-one percent of recruiters use LinkedIn regularly, around eighty-four percent screen candidates on social media, and twenty-one percent have rejected a candidate over their profile. Glassdoor, and aggregated employer surveys, put social-media use in hiring at around ninety-one percent of employers — that's employee-reported, and treated as indicative.

The LinkedIn guidance draws on LinkedIn's official talent and help-centre guidance, and 2026 industry algorithm analyses — profile, keywords, Open to Work, and posting best practice: consistency, comments weighted above likes, dwell time, documents and video. The algorithm is not published, so the specifics are informed guidance. The C-I-P-D provides recruitment, selection, and candidate-experience guidance, including the cautions on social-media screening — fairness, and bias. The National Careers Service, Acas, and major U-K recruiters — Hays, Reed, Michael Page, Robert Walters, Penguin Recruitment, and Carrington West — inform the C-V, cover-letter, interview, and negotiation good practice. The R-T-P-I provides the chartership — M-R-T-P-I — C-P-D, and career-progression guidance. And the I-C-O covers data protection in recruitment.

This guide was compiled in 2026 for PlanRef. Statistics are attributed to their source. Guidance on fast-moving platforms reflects current best practice, and should be re-checked as algorithms and features change. And the craft advice is drawn from long recruitment experience, offered as informed judgement.
